meta.title: Agentic AI Architecture Map
meta.description: A map of all the moving pieces in a production agentic AI system
meta.author: Daniel Rosehill / Carrot Cake AI

layers.label: Prompts
layers.description: The instruction layer — where human intent enters the system. Prompt assembly is itself complex: template rendering, context injection from RAG, tool definition injection, conversation history management, and token budget allocation.

nodes.label: User Prompt
nodes.description: The actual question or instruction from the end user — the starting point of every interaction.

nodes.label: System Prompt
nodes.description: Developer-defined instructions that shape the model's behavior, persona, and constraints. Also the injection point for RAG context and tool definitions.

nodes.label: Vendor Prompt
nodes.description: Hidden instructions baked into the model by the provider — RLHF alignment, safety guardrails, and system-level directives. The model is never truly vanilla, even via API.

layers.label: Models
layers.description: The large language models (or other foundation models) that power reasoning and generation. Includes both generative LLMs and embedding models, which serve architecturally distinct roles.

nodes.label: Commercial
nodes.description: Proprietary frontier models — Claude, GPT, Gemini, Cohere.

nodes.label: Open Source
nodes.description: Open-weight models — Llama, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Phi.

nodes.label: Fine-Tuned
nodes.description: Task-specific models fine-tuned on your data.

nodes.label: Embedding
nodes.description: Embedding models that convert text to vector representations — architecturally distinct from generative LLMs. Power the vector store and RAG pipeline, not agent reasoning.

layers.label: Inference
layers.description: Where and how models are served — the compute layer. In production, requests often route through a model gateway before reaching the endpoint.

nodes.label: Gateway
nodes.description: Model gateway / router — the front door for inference requests. Handles model selection, load balancing, failover, API key management, rate limiting, and cost tracking. OpenRouter (hosted) or LiteLLM (self-hosted). When present, all inference requests route through the gateway before reaching the model endpoint.

nodes.label: Cloud APIs
nodes.description: Hosted API endpoints — Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, AWS Bedrock. When a gateway is used, the gateway routes to these endpoints; without a gateway, agents call them directly.

nodes.label: Self-Hosted
nodes.description: Running models on your own servers — vLLM, TGI, Ollama. A self-hosted LiteLLM gateway can sit in front of these for unified routing.

nodes.label: On-Prem
nodes.description: Models in your data center — air-gapped, regulatory compliance.

nodes.label: Edge
nodes.description: Lightweight models on devices — low latency, offline.

nodes.label: Cache
nodes.description: Prompt and response caching — semantic deduplication, response caching, and embedding caching. Reduces cost and latency. May be built into the gateway or a separate layer.

layers.label: Agents
layers.description: The orchestration and execution layer — where AI systems reason, plan, and act. Agent execution is iterative: the agent calls models and tools in a loop, not once.

nodes.label: Orchestration
nodes.description: Coordination layer — routing tasks to agents, pipelines, or workflows. Handles failures, manages state, and may intelligently select which sub-pattern to invoke.

nodes.label: Agents
nodes.description: Autonomous AI systems that reason, plan, and act. Agents autonomously decide which tools to invoke and when — tool selection is intelligent and non-deterministic. Execution is a loop: reason → act → observe → reason again.

nodes.label: Pipelines
nodes.description: Multi-step data processing chains. Tools are wired in at design time as fixed steps — no intelligent tool selection. The developer decides the sequence, not the model.

nodes.label: Workflows
nodes.description: Event-triggered automated sequences. Like pipelines, tools are pre-configured at each node. Branching may occur, but the tool at each branch is predetermined.

layers.label: MCP
layers.description: The Model Context Protocol — the open standard for connecting AI models to external tools, services, and data sources. Encompasses tool invocation, resource access, and prompt template serving.

nodes.label: Model Context Protocol
nodes.description: The open standard for connecting AI models to external tools, services, and data sources.

nodes.label: Tool Registry
nodes.description: Discovery mechanism for available tools — in MCP this is tools/list. Agents query the registry to learn what tools exist and what they do. Tool descriptions are effectively prompts; too many tools degrade model performance.

layers.label: Human-in-the-Loop
layers.description: Approval checkpoints where humans review and authorize agent actions before execution — the brakes on the system. Pattern varies: per-action for agents, stage-gate for pipelines, exception-based for workflows.

nodes.label: Human-in-the-Loop
nodes.description: Approval checkpoints where humans review and authorize agent actions before execution — the brakes on the system.

layers.label: Actions
layers.description: External systems that agents act on — your data, third-party services, APIs, and financial systems. Bidirectional: agents both read from and write to these systems.

nodes.label: Your Data
nodes.description: Connections to your data — Salesforce, HubSpot, Google Drive, Notion — via authenticated MCP servers.

nodes.label: Search APIs
nodes.description: Web search, news feeds, and real-time data — typically API-key-based MCP integrations.

nodes.label: External Services
nodes.description: Third-party services and public integrations — weather, time, public datasets.

nodes.label: Digital Wallets
nodes.description: Payment and financial actions — Stripe, crypto wallets, bank APIs. Agents can initiate transactions, check balances, and manage subscriptions.

layers.label: Storage
layers.description: Persistence layer — where conversations, outputs, and prompt templates are stored.

nodes.label: Conversations
nodes.description: Persisted conversation threads for auditing, replay, and continuity.

nodes.label: Outputs
nodes.description: Generated text, structured data, files, and artifacts.

nodes.label: Prompts
nodes.description: Prompt templates, system instructions, and versioned prompt libraries.

nodes.label: Postgres
nodes.description: Production relational databases — structured data, transactions, audit trails.

nodes.label: Data Lakes
nodes.description: Large-scale storage for unstructured data — logs, documents, embeddings, raw outputs.

layers.label: Context Store
layers.description: The knowledge layer — RAG pipelines and persistent memory that ground agents in your data.

nodes.label: Context (RAG)
nodes.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation — grounding AI in your documents and knowledge bases.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.description: Persistent memory — multiple forms: mined memory (outputs post-processed into RAG, a transport layer), ad-hoc memory (agent actively writes and reads memory artifacts, e.g. Claude Code's MEMORY.md), implicit memory (conversation history in context window), and structured memory (facts stored in knowledge graphs or key-value stores).

nodes.label: Vector Store
nodes.description: Embedding-based indexes — semantic search over documents, database records, and agent outputs.

layers.label: Frontends
layers.description: The interfaces your users see and interact with — chat windows, bot integrations, and dashboards.

nodes.label: Frontends
nodes.description: The interfaces your users see and interact with — chat windows, bot integrations, and dashboards.

layers.label: Safety
layers.description: Constraints and oversight mechanisms that keep agents operating within defined boundaries. Applied at multiple points: input filtering, agent constraints, tool-level restrictions, output filtering.

nodes.label: Safety
nodes.description: Constraints and oversight mechanisms that keep agents operating within defined boundaries.

layers.label: Observability
layers.description: Tracking decisions, measuring quality, logging actions, and monitoring system health. Feeds back into prompt optimization.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.description: Tracking decisions, measuring quality, logging actions, and monitoring system health.

layers.label: Destinations
layers.description: Where stored data ultimately flows — knowledge management, compliance archives, and prompt libraries.

nodes.label: Prompt Library
nodes.description: A managed, versioned collection of prompt templates — curated from stored prompts for reuse across systems.

nodes.label: Wiki / KM
nodes.description: Knowledge management systems — wikis, Notion, Confluence — populated with curated agent outputs for organizational knowledge.

nodes.label: Data Warehouse
nodes.description: Structured analytical storage for data mining, compliance auditing, and long-term retention of agent activity.

connections.label: INPUT
connections.description: User requests flow from the frontend interface into the prompt layer.
Assembled prompts (user + system + vendor + injected RAG context + tool definitions) are sent to the model.
Selected model is served through the chosen inference path.

Gateway routes requests to cloud API endpoints based on model selection, cost, latency, or fallback rules. OpenRouter routes to Anthropic, OpenAI, Google etc. LiteLLM (self-hosted) provides the same routing with unified API.
Gateway routes to self-hosted model endpoints. A self-hosted LiteLLM instance can route to local vLLM/TGI/Ollama servers alongside cloud APIs, providing unified access.
Inference results feed into the agentic orchestration layer.
Orchestration dispatches tasks to autonomous agents when the task requires intelligent, flexible execution.
Orchestration dispatches tasks to pipelines when the processing steps are known and fixed.
Orchestration dispatches tasks to workflows when execution is event-triggered with predetermined steps.

connections.label: AGENTIC LOOP
connections.description: Agent execution is iterative — after each tool result, the agent returns to the model for another reasoning step. This loop (reason → act → observe → reason) may execute many times per request. This is the defining characteristic of agentic systems.

connections.label: AUTONOMOUS
connections.description: Agents autonomously invoke tools through MCP — the agent reasons about which tool to call and when. Tool selection is intelligent and non-deterministic.

connections.label: DETERMINISTIC
connections.description: Pipelines invoke tools through MCP deterministically — tools are wired in at design time as fixed steps. No intelligent selection; the developer decides the sequence.

connections.label: DETERMINISTIC
connections.description: Workflows invoke tools through MCP deterministically — tools are pre-configured at each workflow node, triggered by events. Branching may occur, but tool selection at each branch is predetermined.

connections.label: TAKING ACTIONS
connections.description: Actions requested via MCP pass through human approval before execution.
Approved actions proceed to external integrations.

connections.label: DATA RETRIEVAL
connections.description: Data retrieved from external systems flows back through MCP to agents. Integrations are bidirectional — agents read from them (query CRM, fetch documents) as much as they write to them.
Agents persist conversations, outputs, and prompt data to storage.

connections.label: CONTEXT-MINING
connections.description: Stored data is processed and indexed into the context store for retrieval. This is the 'mined memory' pattern — memory as a transport layer where outputs are post-processed into retrievable context by a separate pipeline, not by the agent itself.
Mined context and memory feed back into the agent layer — the virtuous knowledge cycle. This is the 'transport layer' pattern: outputs are post-processed, indexed, and retrieved for future runs.

connections.label: AD-HOC MEMORY
connections.description: Agents directly create and retrieve memory artifacts during execution. Unlike mined memory (a transport layer), the agent itself decides what to remember and when to recall it. Example: Claude Code writing and reading MEMORY.md files.

connections.label: OUTPUT
connections.description: Agent responses are delivered back to the user through the frontend.
Safety guardrails constrain agent behavior and actions.
Input guardrails — prompt injection detection, content filtering, and input sanitization before prompts reach the model.
Tool-level safety constraints — restricting which tools can be called, parameter validation, and preventing unauthorized actions.
Observability tools monitor and log agent execution.
Traces, logs, eval results, and metrics are persisted to data lakes for analysis, debugging, and compliance.

connections.label: PROMPT OPTIMIZATION
connections.description: Evaluation results drive prompt optimization — a feedback loop where measured performance informs prompt iteration and versioning.
Client-side telemetry, user feedback (thumbs up/down), and latency metrics flow from frontends to the observability layer.

connections.label: CONTEXT INJECTION
connections.description: RAG results are injected into the system prompt — this is literally how retrieval-augmented generation works. Retrieved context becomes part of the prompt assembly.
Embedding models generate the vector representations stored in the vector store for semantic search and RAG retrieval.
Stored prompt templates are curated into a managed prompt library for reuse and versioning.
Curated agent outputs are published to wikis and knowledge management systems for organizational use.
Agent outputs flow into a data warehouse for data mining, analytics, and compliance archival.
Production database records are indexed into vector stores, feeding the RAG pipeline with structured context.
User prompts are persisted as conversations for auditing, replay, and context mining about the user.

connections.label: USER CONTEXT MINING
connections.description: Conversations are structured and stored in production databases. A dedicated agentic workflow mines these for user context — preferences, patterns, domain knowledge — feeding the vector store for personalized future interactions.
